Utilizing a template-free occlusion identification method, based on inferred 3D geometry and 2D segmentation, yields more accurate mask generation and enhances generalizability compared to template-dependent approaches.

Integrating a broader temporal context (e.g., a larger temporal window size) through feature warping consistently improves the accuracy, semantic plausibility, and consistency of amodal completion results.
originalProblem: Existing 3D reconstruction methods struggle with dynamic human-object interactions due to frequent mutual occlusions and continuous motion, leading to ambiguous visual cues.
Amodal completion methods for static images fail to maintain temporal consistency when applied to video sequences, resulting in unstable or 'wobbly' reconstructions over time.
Developing a robust and generalizable solution for 3D human-object interaction reconstruction from monocular video is challenging without relying on predefined human or object templates.
results: Achieved superior amodal completion, outperforming baselines with an IoU of 61.75% and CLIP score of 27.64 on the BEHAVE dataset, and 70.18% IoU with 27.65 CLIP on the InterCap dataset.

Demonstrated excellent temporal consistency with the lowest Flow Warping Error (e.g., 6.26 x10
	⁻³) on BEHAVE, indicating minimal pixel-level discrepancies between warped and current frames.

Enabled the first animatable and photo-realistic 3D human-object interaction reconstruction from monocular video, yielding higher 3D metrics (PSNR-M 15.43, SSIM-M 0.6240) compared to methods without completion or with basic inpainting.
solution: A Bidirectional Temporal Feature (BTF) Warping mechanism aligns latent features from neighboring frames to the current frame using optical flow, providing rich temporal context.
A Temporal Fusion Attention module adaptively aggregates these temporally aligned features into a unified latent representation for robust amodal completion.

A template-free occlusion identification strategy precisely localizes occluded regions by projecting inferred 3D point clouds to 2D and applying a concave hull algorithm, avoiding reliance on predefined models.
Temporally-aware amodal completion leverages the fused features and precise occlusion masks within a diffusion inpainting pipeline, followed by 3D Gaussian Splatting for animatable 3D reconstruction.
paper_summary.summary: The OTA-3DHOI framework reconstructs photo-realistic, animatable 3D human-object interactions from monocular video. It addresses occlusions and temporal inconsistencies by integrating a template-free occlusion identification strategy with a temporally consistent amodal completion pipeline.

title: Occlusion-Aware Temporally Consistent Amodal Completion for 3D Human-Object Interaction Reconstruction

abstract: Joint reconstruction of human-object interaction marks a significant milestone in comprehending the intricate interrelations between humans and their surrounding environment. Nevertheless, previous optimization methods often struggle to achieve physically plausible reconstruction results due to the lack of prior knowledge about human-object interactions. In this paper, we introduce ScoreHOI, an effective diffusion-based optimizer that introduces diffusion priors for the precise recovery of human-object interactions. By harnessing the controllability within score-guided sampling, the diffusion model can reconstruct a conditional distribution of human and object pose given the image observation and object feature. During inference, the ScoreHOI effectively improves the reconstruction results by guiding the denoising process with specific physical constraints. Furthermore, we propose a contact-driven iterative refinement approach to enhance the contact plausibility and improve the reconstruction accuracy. Extensive evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate ScoreHOI's superior performance over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its ability to achieve a precise and robust improvement in joint human-object interaction reconstruction.

keyInsights: Score-based diffusion models can effectively function as robust optimizers, integrating rich generative priors with explicit physical constraints for complex 3D reconstruction tasks.
Dynamically updating contact masks within an iterative refinement loop is crucial for substantially improving the accuracy and quality of reconstructed contact interactions.
Combining image and geometric features via an IG-Adapter significantly enhances the diffusion model's ability to generate context-aware and observationally consistent 3D human-object interactions.
originalProblem: Reconstructing accurate and physically plausible 3D human-object interactions from monocular images is challenging due to inherent ambiguities.
Existing optimization-based methods often lack comprehensive prior knowledge about interaction patterns and are computationally expensive.
Regression-based methods for 3D reconstruction lack robustness, particularly in scenarios with severe occlusion or depth ambiguity.
results: Achieved superior reconstruction accuracy, including a 9% improvement in Contact F-Score on the BEHAVE benchmark and top performance across various human and object mesh metrics.
Demonstrated enhanced physical plausibility in reconstructed interactions, especially in challenging side-view perspectives, due to the diffusion model's implicit prior knowledge.

Provided significantly improved inference efficiency, operating one to two orders of magnitude faster than previous optimization-based methods, with an expedited configuration yielding a seven-fold speed increase.
solution: A two-stage framework involving an initial affordance-aware regressor for coarse human and object parameter estimation.
A contact-driven iterative refinement (CDIR) process using a score-based diffusion model as a robust optimizer.
Guiding the diffusion model's sampling with physical constraints (human-object contact, object-floor contact, penetration avoidance) and dynamically updated contact masks.
paper_summary.summary: Tsinghua University researchers developed ScoreHOI, a framework that employs score-guided diffusion models as robust optimizers to reconstruct physically plausible 3D human-object interactions from single monocular images. This approach yields a 9% improvement in Contact F-Score on the BEHAVE benchmark and significantly enhances inference efficiency compared to prior optimization-based methods.

title: ScoreHOI: Physically Plausible Reconstruction of Human-Object Interaction via Score-Guided Diffusion

abstract: Fine-grained capturing of 3D HOI boosts human activity understanding and facilitates downstream visual tasks, including action recognition, holistic scene reconstruction, and human motion synthesis. Despite its significance, existing works mostly assume that humans interact with rigid objects using only a few body parts, limiting their scope. In this paper, we address the challenging problem of f-AHOI, wherein the whole human bodies interact with articulated objects, whose parts are connected by movable joints. We present CHAIRS, a large-scale motion-captured f-AHOI dataset, consisting of 16.2 hours of versatile interactions between 46 participants and 81 articulated and rigid sittable objects. CHAIRS provides 3D meshes of both humans and articulated objects during the entire interactive process, as well as realistic and physically plausible full-body interactions. We show the value of CHAIRS with object pose estimation. By learning the geometrical relationships in HOI, we devise the very first model that leverage human pose estimation to tackle the estimation of articulated object poses and shapes during whole-body interactions. Given an image and an estimated human pose, our model first reconstructs the pose and shape of the object, then optimizes the reconstruction according to a learned interaction prior. Under both evaluation settings (e.g., with or without the knowledge of objects' geometries/structures), our model significantly outperforms baselines. We hope CHAIRS will promote the community towards finer-grained interaction understanding. We will make the data/code publicly available.

organization_info.name: Beijing Institute of General Artificial Intelligence (BIGAI)

title: Full-Body Articulated Human-Object Interaction

abstract: Existing hand-object interactions (HOI) methods are largely limited to rigid objects, while 4D reconstruction methods of articulated objects generally require pre-scanning the object or even multi-view videos. It remains an unexplored but significant challenge to reconstruct 4D human-articulated-object interactions from a single monocular RGB video. Fortunately, recent advancements in foundation models present a new opportunity to address this highly ill-posed problem. To this end, we introduce ArtHOI, an optimization-based framework that integrates and refines priors from multiple foundation models. Our key contribution is a suite of novel methodologies designed to resolve the inherent inaccuracies and physical unreality of these priors. In particular, we introduce an Adaptive Sampling Refinement (ASR) method to optimize object's metric scale and pose for grounding its normalized mesh in world space. Furthermore, we propose a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) guided hand-object alignment method, utilizing contact reasoning information as constraints of hand-object mesh composition optimization. To facilitate a comprehensive evaluation, we also contribute two new datasets, ArtHOI-RGBD and ArtHOI-Wild. Extensive experiments validate the robustness and effectiveness of our ArtHOI across diverse objects and interactions. Project: this https URL.

keyInsights: Leveraging rich, diverse priors embedded within large-scale foundation models can provide a computational analogue to human capacity for perceiving complex 4D interactions.

Systematically taming and refining disparate, often inconsistent, priors from multiple foundation models is essential to achieve coherent, physically plausible 4D reconstructions from monocular video.

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), with structured prompting and depth-augmented cues, can reliably infer fine-grained contact information crucial for accurate and physically plausible hand-object alignment.
originalProblem: Reconstructing 4D dynamics of hands interacting with unknown articulated objects from a single monocular RGB video is a highly ill-posed problem due to limited cues and occlusions.
Prior approaches for HOI or articulated object reconstruction often rely on object templates, pre-scans, or multi-view data, limiting applicability to unconstrained "in-the-wild" scenarios.
Directly combining priors from various foundation models results in inconsistencies, lack of metric grounding, and physically implausible hand-object compositions.
results: ArtHOI achieved substantially lower articulated object reconstruction errors on ArtHOI-RGBD (e.g., Chamfer Distance of 3.334mm on CD Drive) and performed comparably to methods requiring pre-scans on existing datasets.
Hand-object interaction alignment demonstrated superior physical plausibility, with Co 2 scores as low as 0.022 on ArtHOI-RGBD, RSRD, and ArtHOI-Wild datasets.

The Adaptive Sampling Refinement (ASR) method achieved a 100% success rate for metric scale and pose estimation, while MLLM-guided contact reasoning reached up to 88.58% accuracy and reduced false positives to 9.81% on in-the-wild videos.
solution: An optimization-based framework, ArtHOI, is introduced to integrate and refine priors from various foundation models for 4D HOI reconstruction from monocular video.
An Adaptive Sampling Refinement (ASR) algorithm is used to robustly determine the metric scale and 6-DoF pose of initial normalized object meshes by reconciling heterogeneous priors.

Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are guided through structured prompting to infer frame-wise contact information, which constrains a joint optimization for physically plausible hand-articulated-object alignment.
paper_summary.summary: ArtHOI is a framework that reconstructs the 4D dynamics of hands interacting with unknown articulated objects from single monocular RGB video, eliminating the need for object templates or pre-scans. It systematically integrates and refines priors from diverse foundation models, achieving robust articulated object reconstruction errors as low as 3.334mm Chamfer Distance and highly plausible hand-object alignment with Co 2 scores down to 0.022.

title: ArtHOI: Taming Foundation Models for Monocular 4D Reconstruction of Hand-Articulated-Object Interactions

abstract: High-fidelity reconstruction of dynamic scenes is an important yet challenging problem. While recent 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) has demonstrated the ability to model temporal dynamics, it couples Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single covariance formulation, which limits its expressiveness for complex motions and often leads to visual artifacts. To address this, we propose VeGaS, a novel velocity-based 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that decouples Gaussian motion and geometry. Specifically, we introduce a Galilean shearing matrix that explicitly incorporates time-varying velocity to flexibly model complex non-linear motions, while strictly isolating the effects of Gaussian motion from the geometry-related conditional Gaussian covariance. Furthermore, a Geometric Deformation Network is introduced to refine Gaussian shapes and orientations using spatio-temporal context and velocity cues, enhancing temporal geometric modeling. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that VeGaS achieves state-of-the-art performance.

keyInsights: A congruence transformation using a Galilean shearing matrix can rigorously decouple Gaussian motion from its intrinsic 3D geometry, as mathematically proven by Schur Complement Invariance.

Separating the representation and optimization of motion (via shearing and time-varying velocity) and geometry (via a dedicated deformation network) enhances expressiveness and fidelity in dynamic scene reconstruction.
Modeling velocity as a continuous, time-variant function through anchors allows for capturing intricate non-linear trajectories, addressing limitations of constant-velocity assumptions.
originalProblem: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) methods couple Gaussian motion and geometric attributes within a single 4D covariance formulation.
This coupling limits the representation of complex non-linear motions and time-varying geometric deformations in dynamic scenes.
Optimization of intertwined motion and geometry often leads to visual artifacts and compromised reconstruction quality due to mutual interference.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the Neu3DV dataset, with a PSNR of 32.68 dB (an absolute gain of 0.67 dB over 4DGS) and an LPIPS score of 0.09 (over 10% reduction).
Demonstrated superior quantitative and qualitative results on the D-NeRF dataset, outperforming all compared methods in challenging monocular dynamic scene reconstruction.

Produced higher fidelity renderings with significantly fewer artifacts, accurately preserving intricate details such as flame patterns, window views, and object textures compared to previous 4DGS methods.
solution: Introduces a motion-geometric decoupled representation using a 4D Galilean shearing matrix, where a time-varying velocity influences spatial position without distorting intrinsic 3D geometry.
Models complex non-linear motions by deriving instantaneous velocities from interpolated 'velocity anchors' across the temporal domain, enabling more intricate trajectories.
Incorporates a lightweight Geometric Deformation Network to independently refine Gaussian scaling and rotation over time, capturing high-frequency non-rigid deformations.
paper_summary.summary: VeGaS proposes a 4D Gaussian Splatting framework that rigorously decouples motion from geometry. This approach, integrating Galilean shearing with a dedicated geometric deformation network, achieves state-of-the-art results in dynamic scene reconstruction, improving PSNR by 0.67 dB and reducing LPIPS by over 10% on the Neu3DV dataset compared to prior methods.

title: Decoupling Motion and Geometry in 4D Gaussian Splatting

abstract: Reconstructing clean, distractor-free 3D scenes from real-world captures remains a significant challenge, particularly in highly dynamic and cluttered settings such as egocentric videos. To tackle this problem, we introduce DeGauss, a simple and robust self-supervised framework for dynamic scene reconstruction based on a decoupled dynamic-static Gaussian Splatting design. DeGauss models dynamic elements with foreground Gaussians and static content with background Gaussians, using a probabilistic mask to coordinate their composition and enable independent yet complementary optimization. DeGauss generalizes robustly across a wide range of real-world scenarios, from casual image collections to long, dynamic egocentric videos, without relying on complex heuristics or extensive supervision. Experiments on benchmarks including NeRF-on-the-go, ADT, AEA, Hot3D, and EPIC-Fields demonstrate that DeGauss consistently outperforms existing methods, establishing a strong baseline for generalizable, distractor-free 3D reconstructionin highly dynamic, interaction-rich environments. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly decoupling dynamic and static scene elements with dedicated Gaussian sets enables more stable and independent optimization compared to entangled representations.
A probabilistic composition mask, derived from foreground Gaussians, promotes unambiguous dynamic-static separation, improving reconstruction quality.

The introduction of a brightness control mechanism enhances the background representation's ability to handle severe illumination changes and non-Lambertian effects without misclassifying dynamic objects.
originalProblem: Existing 3D reconstruction methods, like NeRF and 3DGS, often degrade in dynamic environments, producing artifacts such as "floaters" or blurred representations.
Reconstructing clean 3D scenes from "in-the-wild" data, particularly egocentric videos with continuous interactions, motion blur, and illumination changes, remains challenging.

Prior dynamic scene modeling and distractor-free reconstruction approaches frequently suffer from sensitivity to initialization, computational expense, or poor generalization to complex real-world data.
results: DeGauss establishes new state-of-the-art for distractor-free static scene reconstruction, demonstrating superior LPIPS scores (e.g., 0.113) on datasets like NeRF-on-the-go, indicating cleaner visual quality.

The method significantly improves rendering efficiency for dynamic scenes, achieving substantially higher Frames Per Second (e.g., 157 FPS) while maintaining or enhancing quality compared to other dynamic 3DGS approaches.

Ablation studies validate the critical role of components such as brightness control, partial opacity reset, and specific loss functions in achieving stable training and effective dynamic-static decomposition.
solution: DeGauss proposes a self-supervised framework that explicitly models dynamic elements using foreground Gaussians and static content with background Gaussians.
A probabilistic composition mask and a brightness control mechanism are employed to coordinate the decoupled dynamic and static representations.
The framework utilizes a "compose after rendering" strategy for final scene composition, ensuring robust gradient flow and stable decomposition during training.
paper_summary.summary: DeGauss introduces a self-supervised framework that decouples dynamic and static elements using Gaussian Splatting, yielding distractor-free 3D reconstructions from complex, real-world videos. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance in static scene quality and significantly improves rendering efficiency for dynamic content, especially in challenging egocentric video datasets.

title: DeGauss: Dynamic-Static Decomposition with Gaussian Splatting for Distractor-free 3D Reconstruction

abstract: Real-world human-built environments are highly dynamic, involving multiple humans and their complex interactions with surrounding objects. While 3D geometry modeling of such scenes is crucial for applications like AR/VR, gaming, and embodied AI, it remains underexplored due to challenges like diverse motion patterns and frequent occlusions. Beyond novel view rendering, 3D Gaussian Splatting (GS) has demonstrated remarkable progress in producing detailed, high-quality surface geometry with fast optimization of the underlying structure. However, very few GS-based methods address multihuman, multiobject scenarios, primarily due to the above-mentioned inherent challenges. In a monocular setup, these challenges are further amplified, as maintaining structural consistency under severe occlusion becomes difficult when the scene is optimized solely based on GS-based rendering loss. To tackle the challenges of such a multihuman, multiobject dynamic scene, we propose a hybrid approach that effectively combines the advantages of 1) 3D generative models for generating high-fidelity meshes of the scene elements, 2) Semantic-aware deformation, \ie rigid transformation of the rigid objects and LBS-based deformation of the humans, and mapping of the deformed high-fidelity meshes in the dynamic scene, and 3) GS-based optimization of the individual elements for further refining their alignments in the scene. Such a hybrid approach helps maintain the object structures even under severe occlusion and can produce multiview and temporally consistent geometry. We choose HOI-M3 for evaluation, as, to the best of our knowledge, this is the only dataset featuring multihuman, multiobject interactions in a dynamic scene. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art method in producing better surface reconstruction of such scenes.
title: Asset-Driven Sematic Reconstruction of Dynamic Scene with Multi-Human-Object Interactions

abstract: Existing methods for reconstructing objects and humans from a monocular image suffer from severe mesh collisions and performance limitations for interacting occluding objects. This paper introduces a method to obtain a globally consistent 3D reconstruction of interacting objects and people from a single image. Our contributions include: 1) an optimization framework, featuring a collision loss, tailored to handle human-object and human-human interactions, ensuring spatially coherent scene reconstruction; and 2) a novel technique to robustly estimate 6 degrees of freedom (DOF) poses, specifically for heavily occluded objects, exploiting image inpainting. Notably, our proposed method operates effectively on images from real-world scenarios, without necessitating scene or object-level 3D supervision. Extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluation against existing methods demonstrates a significant reduction in collisions in the final reconstructions of scenes with multiple interacting humans and objects and a more coherent scene reconstruction.
title: Single-image coherent reconstruction of objects and humans

abstract: Monocular dynamic video reconstruction faces significant challenges in dynamic human scenes due to geometric inconsistencies and resolution degradation issues. Existing methods lack 3D human structural understanding, producing geometrically inconsistent results with distorted limb proportions and unnatural human-object fusion, while memory-constrained downsampling causes human boundary drift toward background geometry. To address these limitations, we propose to incorporate hybrid geometric priors that combine SMPL human body models with monocular depth estimation. Our approach leverages structured human priors to maintain surface consistency while capturing fine-grained geometric details in human regions. We introduce HuPrior3R, featuring a hierarchical pipeline with refinement components that processes full-resolution images for overall scene geometry, then applies strategic cropping and cross-attention fusion for human-specific detail enhancement. The method integrates SMPL priors through a Feature Fusion Module to ensure geometrically plausible reconstruction while preserving fine-grained human boundaries. Extensive experiments on TUM Dynamics and GTA-IM datasets demonstrate superior performance in dynamic human reconstruction.

keyInsights: Explicitly incorporating structured human body priors, such as SMPL models, is essential to mitigate anatomical artifacts and achieve geometrically plausible human reconstructions.
Combining complementary geometric information from fine-grained monocular depth maps and structurally consistent SMPL models, with careful alignment, significantly improves reconstruction fidelity.
A hierarchical processing strategy, including a dedicated human-centric refinement module, effectively addresses resolution degradation for small human figures within a larger scene.
originalProblem: Existing 3D dynamic reconstruction methods often produce geometrically implausible human representations with distorted limbs or disconnected body parts due to a lack of structural understanding.
Humans in monocular videos occupy small pixel regions, leading to resolution and boundary degradation during processing as fine details blend with background geometry.
Current approaches to dynamic scene reconstruction typically treat humans as generic, unstructured dynamic objects, not leveraging specific human body knowledge.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art depth estimation on real-world human-centric datasets, with an Abs Rel of 0.033 on BEHAVE and 0.077 on Bonn.
Demonstrated state-of-the-art camera pose estimation, including an Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) of 0.015 on the synthetic GTA-IM dataset.
Ablation studies validated the effectiveness of SMPL priors and the human-centric refinement, showing significant quantitative and qualitative improvements in human detail and temporal consistency.
solution: Proposes a hierarchical pipeline involving global scene processing and a dedicated human-centric refinement module for fine details.
Integrates hybrid geometric priors, including Skinned Multi-Person Linear (SMPL) human body models and monocular depth estimates, to inform human reconstruction.
Employs a Feature Fusion Module and RANSAC-based depth alignment to intelligently combine and ensure consistency between different geometric information sources.
paper_summary.summary: A method called HuPrior3R improves 3D dynamic human reconstruction from monocular videos by incorporating hybrid geometric priors and a hierarchical refinement module. The system effectively mitigates anatomical artifacts and resolution degradation, achieving state-of-the-art results including an Abs Rel of 0.033 on the BEHAVE dataset for depth estimation and an ATE of 0.015 on the GTA-IM dataset for camera pose.

title: HuPrior3R: Incorporating Human Priors for Better 3D Dynamic Reconstruction from Monocular Videos

abstract: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) excels in static scene modeling, its extension to dynamic scenes introduces significant challenges. Existing dynamic 3DGS methods suffer from either over-smoothing due to low-rank decomposition or feature collision from high-dimensional grid sampling. This is because of the inherent spectral conflicts between preserving motion details and maintaining deformation consistency at different frequency. To address these challenges, we propose a novel dynamic 3DGS framework with hybrid explicit-implicit functions. Our approach contains three key innovations: a spectral-aware Laplacian encoding architecture which merges Hash encoding and Laplacian-based module for flexible frequency motion control, an enhanced Gaussian dynamics attribute that compensates for photometric distortions caused by geometric deformation, and an adaptive Gaussian split strategy guided by KDTree-based primitive control to efficiently query and optimize dynamic areas. Through extensive experiments, our method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in reconstructing complex dynamic scenes, achieving better reconstruction fidelity.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

title: Laplacian Analysis Meets Dynamics Modelling: Gaussian Splatting for 4D Reconstruction

abstract: Amodal completion, which is the process of inferring the full appearance of objects despite partial occlusions, is crucial for understanding complex human-object interactions (HOI) in computer vision and robotics. Existing methods, such as those that use pre-trained diffusion models, often struggle to generate plausible completions in dynamic scenarios because they have a limited understanding of HOI. To solve this problem, we've developed a new approach that uses physical prior knowledge along with a specialized multi-regional inpainting technique designed for HOI. By incorporating physical constraints from human topology and contact information, we define two distinct regions: the primary region, where occluded object parts are most likely to be, and the secondary region, where occlusions are less probable. Our multi-regional inpainting method uses customized denoising strategies across these regions within a diffusion model. This improves the accuracy and realism of the generated completions in both their shape and visual detail. Our experimental results show that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods in HOI scenarios, moving machine perception closer to a more human-like understanding of dynamic environments. We also show that our pipeline is robust even without ground-truth contact annotations, which broadens its applicability to tasks like 3D reconstruction and novel view/pose synthesis.
title: Contact-Aware Amodal Completion for Human-Object Interaction via Multi-Regional Inpainting

abstract: This paper addresses a novel task of anticipating 3D human-object interactions (HOIs). Most existing research on HOI synthesis lacks comprehensive whole-body interactions with dynamic objects, e.g., often limited to manipulating small or static objects. Our task is significantly more challenging, as it requires modeling dynamic objects with various shapes, capturing whole-body motion, and ensuring physically valid interactions. To this end, we propose InterDiff, a framework comprising two key steps: (i) interaction diffusion, where we leverage a diffusion model to encode the distribution of future human-object interactions; (ii) interaction correction, where we introduce a physics-informed predictor to correct denoised HOIs in a diffusion step. Our key insight is to inject prior knowledge that the interactions under reference with respect to contact points follow a simple pattern and are easily predictable. Experiments on multiple human-object interaction datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for this task, capable of producing realistic, vivid, and remarkably long-term 3D HOI predictions.

keyInsights: Despite the complexity of global human-object dynamics, the short-term relative motion of an object with respect to its contact point on the human body often follows simpler, more predictable patterns.

Integrating physics-informed correction directly into the iterative denoising process of a diffusion model can effectively ensure physical plausibility without the computational burden of full physics simulations or post-optimization.

Decoupled training of the generative diffusion model and the physics-informed predictor, followed by their seamless composition during inference, offers an efficient and practical approach for complex HOI synthesis.
originalProblem: Existing 3D human-object interaction (HOI) synthesis methods often focus only on hand-object interactions or simplify human representations (skeletons), failing to capture whole-body, mesh-based contact with dynamic objects.

Previous approaches struggled to ensure physical validity (e.g., preventing penetration, maintaining realistic contact) without relying on computationally expensive post-hoc optimization or complex, hard-to-set-up physics simulations.
Generative models for human motion often lack an inherent understanding of physical laws, leading to artifacts like 'floating contact' or object penetration when applied to HOI scenarios.
results: InterDiff consistently outperforms established baselines in quantitative metrics, demonstrating reduced errors in human and object pose prediction and significantly lowering object penetration artifacts (e.g., from 228 to 164 on BEHAVE dataset).
The interaction correction step proved crucial, not only decreasing penetration but also improving the accuracy of predicted human motion due to enhanced object dynamics.

The model shows strong generalizability to unseen objects and cross-dataset transfer (BEHAVE to GRAB) without fine-tuning, and a user study confirmed its superior physical fidelity, with users rating InterDiff's interactions as more realistic 67.8% of the time.
solution: InterDiff introduces a dual-component framework: an Interaction Diffusion model (DDPM-based with a transformer) generates diverse future HOI sequences, and an Interaction Correction component refines these sequences for physical validity.

The Interaction Correction leverages the insight that an object's short-term relative motion to its human contact point is simpler, using a spatial-temporal graph neural network (STGNN) to predict and correct object dynamics.

A judicious correction schedule applies physical refinements (penalizing penetration, promoting contact) only in later diffusion steps and when implausibilities are detected, avoiding unnecessary alterations to otherwise valid motions.
paper_summary.summary: InterDiff, developed by researchers at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, is a learning-based framework that forecasts 3D whole-body human-object interactions with dynamic objects by integrating physics-informed corrections into a diffusion model. The system accurately predicts physically valid and diverse long-term interactions, significantly reducing object penetration and achieving cross-dataset generalizability.

title: InterDiff: Generating 3D Human-Object Interactions with Physics-Informed Diffusion

abstract: We address the challenging task of text-driven 3D human-object interaction (HOI) motion generation. Existing methods primarily rely on a direct text-to-HOI mapping, which suffers from three key limitations due to the significant cross-modality gap: (Q1) sub-optimal human motion, (Q2) unnatural object motion, and (Q3) weak interaction between humans and objects. To address these challenges, we propose MP-HOI, a novel framework grounded in four core insights: (1) Multimodal Data Priors: We leverage multimodal data (text, image, pose/object) from large multimodal models as priors to guide HOI generation, which tackles Q1 and Q2 in data modeling. (2) Enhanced Object Representation: We improve existing object representations by incorporating geometric keypoints, contact features, and dynamic properties, enabling expressive object representations, which tackles Q2 in data representation. (3) Multimodal-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Model: We propose a modality-aware MoE model for effective multimodal feature fusion paradigm, which tackles Q1 and Q2 in feature fusion. (4) Cascaded Diffusion with Interaction Supervision: We design a cascaded diffusion framework that progressively refines human-object interaction features under dedicated supervision, which tackles Q3 in interaction refinement. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MP-HOI outperforms existing approaches in generating high-fidelity and fine-grained HOI motions.

keyInsights: Leveraging structured hierarchical priors from large multimodal models (textual, visual, 3D spatial) effectively bridges the cross-modality gap in text-to-3D HOI generation.
Augmenting object representations with geometric keypoints and dynamic attributes (velocities, contact info) is crucial for generating natural and stable object motions during interaction.

A cascaded diffusion framework combined with a modality-aware Mixture-of-Experts model and specific interaction supervision significantly improves the generation of fine-grained and physically plausible human-object interactions.
originalProblem: Existing text-driven 3D HOI generation methods often produce sub-optimal human motion due to insufficient modeling of textual conditions.
Prior approaches rely on simplified object representations, leading to unnatural object motions like floating or sliding.
Weak interaction motions result from direct, one-step text-to-HOI mapping, failing to capture fine-grained dynamics and physical plausibility.
results: MP-HOI achieved superior R-TOP3 scores and lower FID values (e.g., 23.92% FID reduction on FullBodyManipulation, 66.02% on HIMO 3-object) compared to SOTA methods like HIMO-Gen and MotionDiffuse.

Improvements were observed in interaction-specific metrics, including enhanced contact precision, recall, and F1 score (e.g., C_F1 increased by 4.12% on HIMO 2-object), and a 19.04% reduction in interaction distance (D_I), indicating less interpenetration.

User studies showed a strong preference for MP-HOI's semantic alignment and fine-grained interaction quality, and the model demonstrated generalization to unseen objects not present in the training data.
solution: MP-HOI integrates multimodal priors (1D textual from GPT-4o, 2D visual from Flux, 3D spatial for human actions and object geometry) to provide rich semantic guidance.
It employs an enhanced object representation that includes geometric keypoints, translational and angular velocities, and contact information, providing detailed geometric and dynamic properties.

A cascaded diffusion framework progressively refines motions, first generating coarse human and object movements, then a modality-aware Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model fuses heterogeneous multimodal features, followed by a dedicated HOI diffusion step with comprehensive supervision losses.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Beihang University and the University of Durham developed MP-HOI, a text-driven 3D human-object interaction generation framework that leverages multimodal priors and a cascaded diffusion architecture. The system produces higher fidelity motions with improved semantic consistency and fine-grained interaction quality, achieving FID reductions up to 66.02% on multi-object benchmarks and increasing interaction contact precision compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.

title: Multimodal Priors-Augmented Text-Driven 3D Human-Object Interaction Generation

abstract: Real-world scenes often feature multiple humans interacting with multiple objects in ways that are causal, goal-oriented, or cooperative. Yet existing 3D human-object interaction (HOI) benchmarks consider only a fraction of these complex interactions. To close this gap, we present MMHOI -- a large-scale, Multi-human Multi-object Interaction dataset consisting of images from 12 everyday scenarios. MMHOI offers complete 3D shape and pose annotations for every person and object, along with labels for 78 action categories and 14 interaction-specific body parts, providing a comprehensive testbed for next-generation HOI research. Building on MMHOI, we present MMHOI-Net, an end-to-end transformer-based neural network for jointly estimating human-object 3D geometries, their interactions, and associated actions. A key innovation in our framework is a structured dual-patch representation for modeling objects and their interactions, combined with action recognition to enhance the interaction prediction. Experiments on MMHOI and the recently proposed CORE4D datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in multi-HOI modeling, excelling in both accuracy and reconstruction quality. The MMHOI dataset is publicly available at this https URL.
title: MMHOI: Modeling Complex 3D Multi-Human Multi-Object Interactions

abstract: Most prior works in perceiving 3D humans from images reason human in isolation without their surroundings. However, humans are constantly interacting with the surrounding objects, thus calling for models that can reason about not only the human but also the object and their interaction. The problem is extremely challenging due to heavy occlusions between humans and objects, diverse interaction types and depth ambiguity. In this paper, we introduce CHORE, a novel method that learns to jointly reconstruct the human and the object from a single RGB image. CHORE takes inspiration from recent advances in implicit surface learning and classical model-based fitting. We compute a neural reconstruction of human and object represented implicitly with two unsigned distance fields, a correspondence field to a parametric body and an object pose field. This allows us to robustly fit a parametric body model and a 3D object template, while reasoning about interactions. Furthermore, prior pixel-aligned implicit learning methods use synthetic data and make assumptions that are not met in the real data. We propose a elegant depth-aware scaling that allows more efficient shape learning on real data. Experiments show that our joint reconstruction learned with the proposed strategy significantly outperforms the SOTA. Our code and models are available at this https URL
title: CHORE: Contact, Human and Object REconstruction from a single RGB image

abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos remains a fundamental challenge in 3D vision. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves real-time rendering in static settings, extending it to dynamic scenes is challenging due to the difficulty of learning structured and temporally consistent motion representations. This challenge often manifests as three limitations in existing methods: redundant Gaussian updates, insufficient motion supervision, and weak modeling of complex non-rigid deformations. These issues collectively hinder coherent and efficient dynamic reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose HAIF-GS, a unified framework that enables structured and consistent dynamic modeling through sparse anchor-driven deformation. It first identifies motion-relevant regions via an Anchor Filter to suppress redundant updates in static areas. A self-supervised Induced Flow-Guided Deformation module induces anchor motion using multi-frame feature aggregation, eliminating the need for explicit flow labels. To further handle fine-grained deformations, a Hierarchical Anchor Propagation mechanism increases anchor resolution based on motion complexity and propagates multi-level transformations. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks validate that HAIF-GS significantly outperforms prior dynamic 3DGS methods in rendering quality, temporal coherence, and reconstruction efficiency.
title: HAIF-GS: Hierarchical and Induced Flow-Guided Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene

abstract: We introduce HOSNeRF, a novel 360° free-viewpoint rendering method that reconstructs neural radiance fields for dynamic human-object-scene from a single monocular in-the-wild video. Our method enables pausing the video at any frame and rendering all scene details (dynamic humans, objects, and backgrounds) from arbitrary viewpoints. The first challenge in this task is the complex object motions in human-object interactions, which we tackle by introducing the new object bones into the conventional human skeleton hierarchy to effectively estimate large object deformations in our dynamic human-object model. The second challenge is that humans interact with different objects at different times, for which we introduce two new learnable object state embeddings that can be used as conditions for learning our human-object representation and scene representation, respectively. Extensive experiments show that HOSNeRF significantly outperforms SOTA approaches on two challenging datasets by a large margin of 40% ~ 50% in terms of LPIPS. The code, data, and compelling examples of 360° free-viewpoint renderings from single videos will be released in this https URL.

keyInsights: Extending the traditional human skeleton with specialized 'object bones' allows for accurate representation and estimation of drastic object deformations and relative motions during human interactions.
A single, state-conditional neural radiance field, guided by learnable embeddings, can effectively adapt to and represent diverse temporal object states and interactions across a video.
Combining a forward-backward deformation framework with cycle consistency and optical flow supervision significantly improves the robustness and photorealism of dynamic human-object reconstruction.
originalProblem: Existing dynamic NeRF approaches struggle to model complex, non-rigid object motions that occur during human-object interactions.
Current methods find it challenging to represent dynamic scenes where humans interact with objects that change over time, such as being picked up or put down.
Reconstructing high-fidelity 360° free-viewpoint dynamic human-object-scene interactions from a single monocular video remains an unsolved challenge.
results: HOSNeRF demonstrated superior quantitative performance, achieving an average LPIPS gain of approximately 40% on the HOSNeRF dataset and 50% on the NeuMan dataset compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

The method successfully generated high-fidelity, 360° free-viewpoint renderings of dynamic humans, interacting objects, and backgrounds from single 'in-the-wild' monocular videos, with visual quality closely resembling ground truth.

Ablation studies confirmed that the introduced object bones and state embeddings are critical components for effectively handling complex object deformations and temporal object state changes, respectively.
solution: HOSNeRF introduces 'object bones' into the human skeleton hierarchy, along with learnable Linear Blend Skinning (LBS) weights, to effectively model large and complex object deformations during interactions.

It employs state-conditional human-object and scene representations, using learnable state embeddings to unify the modeling of temporal object state changes (e.g., objects appearing or disappearing) within a single framework.

A three-stage training process combines scene, human-object model training, and finetuning, incorporating deformation cycle consistency and optical flow supervision for enhanced accuracy and smoothness.
paper_summary.summary: HOSNeRF, a framework from researchers at the National University of Singapore and Tencent PCG, reconstructs dynamic human-object-scene interactions from a single monocular video. It generates high-fidelity 360° free-viewpoint renderings of dynamic humans, interacting objects, and backgrounds, achieving up to a 50% improvement in LPIPS over existing methods.

title: HOSNeRF: Dynamic Human-Object-Scene Neural Radiance Fields from a Single Video

abstract: 3D human motion capture from monocular RGB images respecting interactions of a subject with complex and possibly deformable environments is a very challenging, ill-posed and under-explored problem. Existing methods address it only weakly and do not model possible surface deformations often occurring when humans interact with scene surfaces. In contrast, this paper proposes MoCapDeform, i.e., a new framework for monocular 3D human motion capture that is the first to explicitly model non-rigid deformations of a 3D scene for improved 3D human pose estimation and deformable environment reconstruction. MoCapDeform accepts a monocular RGB video and a 3D scene mesh aligned in the camera space. It first localises a subject in the input monocular video along with dense contact labels using a new raycasting based strategy. Next, our human-environment interaction constraints are leveraged to jointly optimise global 3D human poses and non-rigid surface deformations. MoCapDeform achieves superior accuracy than competing methods on several datasets, including our newly recorded one with deforming background scenes.
title: MoCapDeform: Monocular 3D Human Motion Capture in Deformable Scenes

abstract:
3D Gaussian Splatting has achieved remarkable success in reconstructing both
static and dynamic 3D scenes. However, in a scene represented by 3D Gaussian
primitives, interactions between objects suffer from inaccurate 3D
segmentation, imprecise deformation among different materials, and severe
rendering artifacts. To address these challenges, we introduce PIG:
Physically-Based Multi-Material Interaction with 3D Gaussians, a novel approach
that combines 3D object segmentation with the simulation of interacting objects
in high precision. Firstly, our method facilitates fast and accurate mapping
from 2D pixels to 3D Gaussians, enabling precise 3D object-level segmentation.
Secondly, we assign unique physical properties to correspondingly segmented
objects within the scene for multi-material coupled interactions. Finally, we
have successfully embedded constraint scales into deformation gradients,
specifically clamping the scaling and rotation properties of the Gaussian
primitives to eliminate artifacts and achieve geometric fidelity and visual
consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that our method not only
outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) in terms of visual quality, but also
opens up new directions and pipelines for the field of physically realistic
scene generation.
title: PIG: Physically-based Multi-Material Interaction with 3D Gaussians

abstract: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes with photorealistic detail and strong temporal coherence remains a significant challenge. Existing Gaussian splatting approaches for dynamic scene modeling often rely on per-frame optimization, which can overfit to instantaneous states instead of capturing underlying motion dynamics. To address this, we present Mango-GS, a multi-frame, node-guided framework for high-fidelity 4D reconstruction. Mango-GS leverages a temporal Transformer to model motion dependencies within a short window of frames, producing temporally consistent deformations. For efficiency, temporal modeling is confined to a sparse set of control nodes. Each node is represented by a decoupled canonical position and a latent code, providing a stable semantic anchor for motion propagation and preventing correspondence drift under large motion. Our framework is trained end-to-end, enhanced by an input masking strategy and two multi-frame losses to improve robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Mango-GS achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and real-time rendering speed, enabling high-fidelity reconstruction and interactive rendering of dynamic scenes.
title: Mango-GS: Enhancing Spatio-Temporal Consistency in Dynamic Scenes Reconstruction using Multi-Frame Node-Guided 4D Gaussian Splatting

abstract: Previous surface reconstruction methods either suffer from low geometric accuracy or lengthy training times when dealing with real-world complex dynamic scenes involving multi-person activities, and human-object interactions. To tackle the dynamic contents and the occlusions in complex scenes, we present a space-time 2D Gaussian Splatting approach. Specifically, to improve geometric quality in dynamic scenes, we learn canonical 2D Gaussian splats and deform these 2D Gaussian splats while enforcing the disks of the Gaussian located on the surface of the objects by introducing depth and normal regularizers. Further, to tackle the occlusion issues in complex scenes, we introduce a compositional opacity deformation strategy, which further reduces the surface recovery of those occluded areas. Experiments on real-world sparse-view video datasets and monocular dynamic datasets demonstrate that our reconstructions outperform state-of-the-art methods, especially for the surface of the details. The project page and more visualizations can be found at: this https URL.
title: Space-time 2D Gaussian Splatting for Accurate Surface Reconstruction under Complex Dynamic Scenes

abstract: This paper focuses on a challenging setting of simultaneously modeling geometry and appearance of hand-object interaction scenes without any object priors. We follow the trend of dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting based methods, and address several significant challenges. To model complex hand-object interaction with mutual occlusion and edge blur, we present interaction-aware hand-object Gaussians with newly introduced optimizable parameters aiming to adopt piecewise linear hypothesis for clearer structural representation. Moreover, considering the complementarity and tightness of hand shape and object shape during interaction dynamics, we incorporate hand information into object deformation field, constructing interaction-aware dynamic fields to model flexible motions. To further address difficulties in the optimization process, we propose a progressive strategy that handles dynamic regions and static background step by step. Correspondingly, explicit regularizations are designed to stabilize the hand-object representations for smooth motion transition, physical interaction reality, and coherent lighting. Experiments show that our approach surpasses existing dynamic 3D-GS-based methods and achieves state-of-the-art performance in reconstructing dynamic hand-object interaction.

keyInsights: Explicitly incorporating learnable parameters (weight and radius) within the Gaussian representation effectively models mutual occlusion and sharp interaction boundaries during complex hand-object interactions.
Augmenting the object deformation field with hand positional information allows for more precise modeling of interaction-induced object changes, capturing local deformations beyond global offsets.

A combination of scene decomposition, interaction-aware Gaussian representation, and 3D geometric regularizations, along with a progressive optimization, is crucial for robustly reconstructing dynamic HOI without object priors.
originalProblem: Existing dynamic hand-object interaction (HOI) reconstruction methods often rely on costly object shape or pose priors, limiting their generalizability to unknown objects.
Generic dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) extensions struggle with the extreme complexities of HOI, such as heavy mutual occlusion, drastic motions, and blurring at interaction boundaries.
Conventional deformation models often fail to adequately disentangle hand and object dynamics or capture fine-grained, interaction-induced object deformations.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art quantitative performance on HOI4D and HO3D datasets, with a significant +9% PSNR gain (30.32 dB) over the best baseline (Deform3DGS at 26.33 dB) on HOI4D translational scenes.

Produced superior qualitative results, demonstrating enhanced appearance and shape fidelity, reduced occlusion artifacts and blurring at interaction boundaries, and robust handling of complex motions like irregular flipping and finger flexibility.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of the interaction-aware module, HOI refinement, and explicit 3D losses, validating their roles in improving accuracy, stability, and physical plausibility.
solution: Decomposes the scene into Hand, Object, and Background Gaussians, introducing a novel Interaction-Aware Hand-Object Gaussian representation with learnable weight and radius parameters to explicitly model mutual occlusion and edge sharpness.

Employs separate implicit deformation fields for hand, object (augmented with hand positional information), and background, enabling interaction-aware transformations and stable background representation.

Utilizes explicit 3D regularizations (hand translation, object rotation, and interaction losses) guided by coarse 3D hand poses, combined with a five-phase progressive optimization strategy for stable and accurate reconstruction.
paper_summary.summary: An Interaction-Aware 4D Gaussian Splatting method reconstructs dynamic hand-object interactions from egocentric videos, achieving high-fidelity geometry and appearance without relying on object shape priors. The approach robustly handles severe mutual occlusion and drastic motions, demonstrating superior reconstruction quality on HOI4D and HO3D datasets with up to a 9% PSNR increase over prior state-of-the-art.

title: Interaction-Aware 4D Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Hand-Object Interaction Reconstruction

abstract: We propose HoliGS, a novel deformable Gaussian splatting framework that addresses embodied view synthesis from long monocular RGB videos. Unlike prior 4D Gaussian splatting and dynamic NeRF pipelines, which struggle with training overhead in minute-long captures, our method leverages invertible Gaussian Splatting deformation networks to reconstruct large-scale, dynamic environments accurately. Specifically, we decompose each scene into a static background plus time-varying objects, each represented by learned Gaussian primitives undergoing global rigid transformations, skeleton-driven articulation, and subtle non-rigid deformations via an invertible neural flow. This hierarchical warping strategy enables robust free-viewpoint novel-view rendering from various embodied camera trajectories by attaching Gaussians to a complete canonical foreground shape (\eg, egocentric or third-person follow), which may involve substantial viewpoint changes and interactions between multiple actors. Our experiments demonstrate that \ourmethod~ achieves superior reconstruction quality on challenging datasets while significantly reducing both training and rendering time compared to state-of-the-art monocular deformable NeRFs. These results highlight a practical and scalable solution for EVS in real-world scenarios. The source code will be released.

keyInsights: Decomposing complex dynamic motion into global rigid, articulated, and local soft deformation components via an invertible warping strategy efficiently models long-duration scenes.
Pre-training with a neural Signed Distance Field (SDF) provides a robust warm-start for Gaussian primitives, crucial for stable and accurate optimization in dynamic environments.
A comprehensive loss function, including photometric, depth, normal, segmentation, and cycle-consistency terms, ensures high-fidelity reconstruction of both appearance and geometry.
originalProblem: Existing dynamic NeRF and 4D Gaussian Splatting methods are computationally expensive for minute-long monocular videos of dynamic scenes.
Current deformable Gaussian Splatting techniques struggle with complex, non-rigid deformations and multi-actor interactions, leading to artifacts and inconsistent reconstructions.
Embodied View Synthesis (EVS) from monocular video faces challenges from extreme viewpoint changes, occlusions, and reliance on fragile external point-tracking models.
results: HoliGS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in visual quality (LPIPS, PSNR, SSIM) and depth accuracy (Acc@0.1m, RMS depth error) on complex human-animal interaction datasets.
It achieves significantly faster training times (approximately 1.2 hours, 10x speedup over comparable SOTA) and near real-time rendering at 20 frames per second.

Ablation studies confirm the critical role of each component, with substantial performance degradation observed when initialization, depth/normal supervision, or hierarchical deformation mechanisms are removed.
solution: HoliGS proposes a hierarchical 4D representation for dynamic scenes, modeling static backgrounds and deformable objects with Gaussian Splatting primitives.
It employs a two-stage invertible dynamic warping strategy, combining rigid SE(3) transformations for global motion with skeleton-driven, Dual-Quaternion Blend Skinning for articulated movements.
An invertible RealNVP flow-based soft deformation field, enforced by a 3D cycle-consistency loss, captures subtle surface changes without requiring external trackers.
paper_summary.summary: HoliGS, a framework for embodied view synthesis from long monocular RGB videos, reconstructs and synthesizes dynamic 3D scenes with superior photometric and geometric quality. It achieves up to 10x faster training and near real-time rendering at 20 frames per second compared to current state-of-the-art methods.

title: HoliGS: Holistic Gaussian Splatting for Embodied View Synthesis

abstract:
4D modeling of human-object interactions is critical for numerous
applications. However, efficient volumetric capture and rendering of complex
interaction scenarios, especially from sparse inputs, remain challenging. In
this paper, we propose NeuralHOFusion, a neural approach for volumetric
human-object capture and rendering using sparse consumer RGBD sensors. It
marries traditional non-rigid fusion with recent neural implicit modeling and
blending advances, where the captured humans and objects are layerwise
disentangled. For geometry modeling, we propose a neural implicit inference
scheme with non-rigid key-volume fusion, as well as a template-aid robust
object tracking pipeline. Our scheme enables detailed and complete geometry
generation under complex interactions and occlusions. Moreover, we introduce a
layer-wise human-object texture rendering scheme, which combines volumetric and
image-based rendering in both spatial and temporal domains to obtain
photo-realistic results. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness
and efficiency of our approach in synthesizing photo-realistic free-view
results under complex human-object interactions.
title: NeuralHOFusion: Neural Volumetric Rendering under Human-object  Interactions

abstract: Accent normalization converts foreign-accented speech into native-like speech while preserving speaker identity. We propose a novel pipeline using self-supervised discrete tokens and non-parallel training data. The system extracts tokens from source speech, converts them through a dedicated model, and synthesizes the output using flow matching. Our method demonstrates superior performance over a frame-to-frame baseline in naturalness, accentedness reduction, and timbre preservation across multiple English accents. Through token-level phonetic analysis, we validate the effectiveness of our token-based approach. We also develop two duration preservation methods, suitable for applications such as dubbing.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: Self-supervised discrete tokens, when deduplicated, effectively isolate phonetic content, allowing the accent conversion model to focus purely on accent mapping and reduce artifacts from synthetic training data.
Integrating flow matching for Mel-spectrogram synthesis enables high-quality, non-autoregressive acoustic generation while preserving speaker identity.
Modular training of the token conversion model and the synthesizer allows for independent optimization, improving flexibility and reducing error propagation between stages.
originalProblem: Existing accent normalization (AN) methods often require expensive parallel speech data or reference speech during inference, which are difficult and costly to obtain.

Previous non-parallel AN approaches relying on TTS-synthesized targets suffer from quality limitations such as inaccurate voice cloning, duration modeling issues, and error accumulation from synthetic data.
Maintaining the original utterance duration is critical for applications like movie dubbing, but existing AN systems struggled with precise duration control.
results: The proposed system achieved higher subjective naturalness (60.38 vs. 48.76) and speaker similarity (0.040 vs. -0.031) compared to a diffusion-based baseline.
Accentedness was significantly reduced (26.14 vs. 37.61), leading to improved intelligibility with a lower Word Error Rate (16.25% vs. 21.54%) on a native ASR model.
A duration control method successfully reduced total utterance duration deviation to 1.64% from the source, demonstrating precise timing preservation for applications like dubbing.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of BART-style pre-training, phone supervision, and accent embeddings for robust performance.
solution: A novel pipeline uses HuBERT-extracted self-supervised discrete tokens, which capture phonetic content, with deduplication to minimize paralinguistic and duration information.

An autoregressive Transformer converts source-accented discrete tokens to native-normalized tokens, trained on non-parallel data with synthetic targets and enhanced by accent conditioning (AdaLN) and BART-style pre-training.

A non-autoregressive flow-matching synthesizer generates Mel-spectrograms from the normalized tokens, preserving speaker identity via speaker embeddings and offering explicit duration control methods for precise utterance timing.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CUHK-Shenzhen, Nanjing University, Tencent, and NUS developed an accent normalization system that leverages self-supervised discrete tokens and flow matching to convert foreign-accented speech to native-like speech using non-parallel data. This approach demonstrated enhanced naturalness, reduced accentedness, and improved speaker identity preservation over prior methods, incorporating effective duration control.

title: Accent Normalization Using Self-Supervised Discrete Tokens with Non-Parallel Data

abstract: Capturing the interactions between humans and their environment in 3D is important for many applications in robotics, graphics, and vision. Recent works to reconstruct the 3D human and object from a single RGB image do not have consistent relative translation across frames because they assume a fixed depth. Moreover, their performance drops significantly when the object is occluded. In this work, we propose a novel method to track the 3D human, object, contacts between them, and their relative translation across frames from a single RGB camera, while being robust to heavy occlusions. Our method is built on two key insights. First, we condition our neural field reconstructions for human and object on per-frame SMPL model estimates obtained by pre-fitting SMPL to a video sequence. This improves neural reconstruction accuracy and produces coherent relative translation across frames. Second, human and object motion from visible frames provides valuable information to infer the occluded object. We propose a novel transformer-based neural network that explicitly uses object visibility and human motion to leverage neighbouring frames to make predictions for the occluded frames. Building on these insights, our method is able to track both human and object robustly even under occlusions. Experiments on two datasets show that our method significantly improves over the state-of-the-art methods. Our code and pretrained models are available at: this https URL

keyInsights: Conditioning neural implicit field reconstructions on temporally consistent human pose estimates (SMPL-T) in camera space is crucial for resolving depth-scale ambiguity and achieving coherent 4D tracking.
Robustly inferring occluded object poses can be achieved by leveraging human motion as a contextual prior and incorporating object visibility scores within a temporal prediction framework (HVOP-Net).
A visibility-aware joint optimization objective, which weights data terms based on predicted object visibility, allows the system to rely more on temporal regularization for heavily occluded frames.
originalProblem: Monocular 3D human-object interaction (HOI) capture suffers from depth-scale ambiguity, leading to inconsistent relative translation between the human, object, and camera across video frames.

Existing monocular methods are fragile when parts of the human or object are heavily occluded, often failing to produce accurate or stable reconstructions due to insufficient single-frame visual evidence.
results: VisTracker consistently outperforms baseline methods (PHOSA, CHORE) on BEHAVE and InterCap datasets, reducing 4D human and object Chamfer distances, with object error on BEHAVE decreasing from 20.32cm (CHORE) to 8.49cm for 10-second window alignment.

The system demonstrates robustness to heavy occlusions, accurately tracking object poses where prior single-frame methods fail, as shown in qualitative comparisons and ablation studies highlighting HVOP-Net's effectiveness.

Ablation studies confirm that both SMPL-T conditioning for consistent translation and HVOP-Net for occlusion handling are critical components, with their removal leading to significant increases in tracking errors.
solution: The method introduces SMPL-T, a sequence-level optimization that refines human body model (SMPL) parameters in camera space to ensure temporally consistent relative translation across an entire video sequence.

A SMPL-T Conditioned Interaction Field Network (SIF-Net) jointly models 3D human and object surfaces and their interactions, using both image features and a novel triplane representation of the SMPL-T mesh.

A Human and Visibility Aware Object Pose Network (HVOP-Net) uses a multi-transformer architecture to infer occluded object poses by leveraging human motion cues and visible object pose information from neighboring frames.
paper_summary.summary: VisTracker, developed by researchers at the University of Tübingen and Max Planck Institute for Informatics, introduces a method for robust 4D human-object interaction tracking from a single RGB camera. It achieves temporally consistent relative translation and accurate object pose estimation even under heavy occlusions by leveraging conditioned neural implicit fields and a human-motion-aware temporal network.

title: Visibility Aware Human-Object Interaction Tracking from Single RGB Camera

abstract: Human-object interaction (HOI) detection is essential for accurately localizing and characterizing interactions between humans and objects, providing a comprehensive understanding of complex visual scenes across various domains. However, existing HOI detectors often struggle to deliver reliable predictions efficiently, relying on resource-intensive training methods and inefficient architectures. To address these challenges, we conceptualize a wavelet attention-like backbone and a novel ray-based encoder architecture tailored for HOI detection. Our wavelet backbone addresses the limitations of expressing middle-order interactions by aggregating discriminative features from the low- and high-order interactions extracted from diverse convolutional filters. Concurrently, the ray-based encoder facilitates multi-scale attention by optimizing the focus of the decoder on relevant regions of interest and mitigating computational overhead. As a result of harnessing the attenuated intensity of learnable ray origins, our decoder aligns query embeddings with emphasized regions of interest for accurate predictions. Experimental results on benchmark datasets, including ImageNet and HICO-DET, showcase the potential of our proposed architecture. The code is publicly available at [this https URL].
title: Conceptualizing Multi-scale Wavelet Attention and Ray-based Encoding for Human-Object Interaction Detection

abstract: We present Orientation-anchored Gaussian Splatting (OriGS), a novel framework for high-quality 4D reconstruction from casually captured monocular videos. While recent advances extend 3D Gaussian Splatting to dynamic scenes via various motion anchors, such as graph nodes or spline control points, they often rely on low-rank assumptions and fall short in modeling complex, region-specific deformations inherent to unconstrained dynamics. OriGS addresses this by introducing a hyperdimensional representation grounded in scene orientation. We first estimate a Global Orientation Field that propagates principal forward directions across space and time, serving as stable structural guidance for dynamic modeling. Built upon this, we propose Orientation-aware Hyper-Gaussian, a unified formulation that embeds time, space, geometry, and orientation into a coherent probabilistic state. This enables inferring region-specific deformation through principled conditioned slicing, adaptively capturing diverse local dynamics in alignment with global motion intent. Experiments demonstrate the superior reconstruction fidelity of OriGS over mainstream methods in challenging real-world dynamic scenes.
title: Orientation-anchored Hyper-Gaussian for 4D Reconstruction from Casual Videos

abstract:
Video-based Human-Object Interaction (HOI) recognition explores the intricate
dynamics between humans and objects, which are essential for a comprehensive
understanding of human behavior and intentions. While previous work has made
significant strides, effectively integrating geometric and visual features to
model dynamic relationships between humans and objects in a graph framework
remains a challenge. In this work, we propose a novel end-to-end category to
scenery framework, CATS, starting by generating geometric features for various
categories through graphs respectively, then fusing them with corresponding
visual features. Subsequently, we construct a scenery interactive graph with
these enhanced geometric-visual features as nodes to learn the relationships
among human and object categories. This methodological advance facilitates a
deeper, more structured comprehension of interactions, bridging
category-specific insights with broad scenery dynamics. Our method demonstrates
state-of-the-art performance on two pivotal HOI benchmarks, including the
MPHOI-72 dataset for multi-person HOIs and the single-person HOI CAD-120
dataset.
title: From Category to Scenery: An End-to-End Framework for Multi-Person  Human-Object Interaction Recognition in Videos

abstract: Human-Object Interaction (HOI) recognition in videos requires understanding both visual patterns and geometric relationships as they evolve over time. Visual and geometric features offer complementary strengths. Visual features capture appearance context, while geometric features provide structural patterns. Effectively fusing these multimodal features without compromising their unique characteristics remains challenging. We observe that establishing robust, entity-specific representations before modeling interactions helps preserve the strengths of each modality. Therefore, we hypothesize that a bottom-up approach is crucial for effective multimodal fusion. Following this insight, we propose the Geometric Visual Fusion Graph Neural Network (GeoVis-GNN), which uses dual-attention feature fusion combined with interdependent entity graph learning. It progressively builds from entity-specific representations toward high-level interaction understanding. To advance HOI recognition to real-world scenarios, we introduce the Concurrent Partial Interaction Dataset (MPHOI-120). It captures dynamic multi-person interactions involving concurrent actions and partial engagement. This dataset helps address challenges like complex human-object dynamics and mutual occlusions. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method across various HOI scenarios. These scenarios include two-person interactions, single-person activities, bimanual manipulations, and complex concurrent partial interactions. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.
title: Geometric Visual Fusion Graph Neural Networks for Multi-Person Human-Object Interaction Recognition in Videos

abstract: Reconstructing object deformation from a single image remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods typically rely on multi-view video to recover deformation, limiting their applicability under constrained scenarios. To address this, we propose DeformSplat, a novel framework that effectively guides 3D Gaussian deformation from only a single image. Our method introduces two main technical contributions. First, we present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching which bridges the domain gap between 3D Gaussian representations and 2D pixel observations. This enables robust deformation guidance from sparse visual cues. Second, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation consisting of initialization and refinement. This segmentation explicitly identifies rigid regions, crucial for maintaining geometric coherence during deformation. By combining these two techniques, our approach can reconstruct consistent deformations from a single image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods and naturally extends to various applications,such as frame interpolation and interactive object manipulation.
title: Rigidity-Aware 3D Gaussian Deformation from a Single Image

abstract: Reconstructing an interactive human avatar and the background from a monocular video of a dynamic human scene is highly challenging. In this work we adopt a strategy of point cloud decoupling and joint optimization to achieve the decoupled reconstruction of backgrounds and human bodies while preserving the interactivity of human motion. We introduce a position texture to subdivide the Skinned Multi-Person Linear (SMPL) body model's surface and grow the human point cloud. To capture fine details of human dynamics and deformations, we incorporate a convolutional neural network structure to predict human body point cloud features based on texture. This strategy makes our approach free of hyperparameter tuning for densification and efficiently represents human points with half the point cloud of HUGS. This approach ensures high-quality human reconstruction and reduces GPU resource consumption during training. As a result, our method surpasses the previous state-of-the-art HUGS in reconstruction metrics while maintaining the ability to generalize to novel poses and views. Furthermore, our technique achieves real-time rendering at over 100 FPS, $\sim$6$\times$ the HUGS speed using only Linear Blend Skinning (LBS) weights for human transformation. Additionally, this work demonstrates that this framework can be extended to animal scene reconstruction when an accurately-posed model of an animal is available.

organization_info.name: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology

title: SkinningGS: Editable Dynamic Human Scene Reconstruction Using Gaussian Splatting Based on a Skinning Model

abstract: Accurately modeling detailed interactions between human/hand and object is an appealing yet challenging task. Current multi-view capture systems are only capable of reconstructing multiple subjects into a single, unified mesh, which fails to model the states of each instance individually during interactions. To address this, previous methods use template-based representations to track human/hand and object. However, the quality of the reconstructions is limited by the descriptive capabilities of the templates so that these methods are inherently struggle with geometry details, pressing deformations and invisible contact surfaces. In this work, we propose an end-to-end Instance-aware Human-Object Interactions recovery (Ins-HOI) framework by introducing an instance-level occupancy field representation. However, the real-captured data is presented as a holistic mesh, unable to provide instance-level supervision. To address this, we further propose a complementary training strategy that leverages synthetic data to introduce instance-level shape priors, enabling the disentanglement of occupancy fields for different instances. Specifically, synthetic data, created by randomly combining individual scans of humans/hands and objects, guides the network to learn a coarse prior of instances.
